chapters.title: Why Watergate still matters
chapters.text:
The Watergate scandal remains one of the biggest political scandals in the history of the United States and of the wider world.

The Watergate affair, which led to the fall of a number of powerful figures around the President, also led to the resignation of a US President in the face of impeachment. When five burglars were caught at the Democratic Party Headquarters in 1972, no one could have predicted that it would set off a chain of events that would shake the political system and force a president to step down.

For democracy to work, a nation has to be willing to uphold the rule of law.

Lead journalists and investigators have told the story in such detail that almost every piece is on the record. In what many call the sharpest investigation of its kind, Bob Woodward and Carl Bernstein put on a masterclass and produced a landmark book.

The details of the scandal are central to the history of the United States and to the world at large. The conspiracies that followed, and the global press coverage, gave new life to the way people see their government. Public trust in politicians dropped, and democracy entered a new phase in which citizens demanded more transparency from their leaders.

In this summary, you will find a thorough look at the investigative work of Carl Bernstein and Bob Woodward. The political fallout from the scandal, and its economic effects, are also explained. Whatever you want to know about Watergate, this short summary is a must read.

chapters.title: A single phone call that started it all
chapters.text:
Bob Woodward got a phone call at about 9 a.m. on June 19, 1972. He was told that five men had entered the Democratic National Committee's headquarters at the Watergate office, apartment, and hotel complex and taken over the building.

There is too little patience and context in many of the investigations I read or see on television.

Woodward was not happy with the assignment at first, since he thought the case would not give him much to write about. The moment he walked into the newsroom, he learned that the burglary had been aimed directly at the Democratic Party's headquarters. He also learned that he would be working with another reporter, Carl Bernstein, who was covering the same story. It was clear from the start that the two men did not get along and had little respect for each other's reporting. The media had trusted them to find the real story behind the break in, because some of the facts did not line up. When the burglars were caught, they did not look worried, and they had even lined up a lawyer to represent them.

The attitude and faces of the thieves at the Watergate Hotel suggested that the situation was more serious than a typical burglary.

Using many names and several approaches to draw out information from inside sources, Woodward and Bernstein figured out that Howard Hunt, a White House consultant, was most likely the inside contact for the burglars. They learned that the thieves had come to the headquarters to plant wiretapping equipment and spy on the employees. Even in what should have been a simple case, the level of preparation and the calm attitude of the thieves showed that there was more to it than met the eye.

In the Watergate scandal, 69 people were charged, and 48 were found guilty.

chapters.title: Nixon’s campaign team and a hidden agenda
chapters.text:
Woodward started his research by looking for a possible Hunt to Colson link. The President's Special Counsel, Charles Colson, was part of the plot, even though the President publicly denied that his staff had any role. Hunt vanished after Woodward called him. Woodward kept at it by calling Newsweek to find out more about Gordon Liddy, the Finance Counsel, who had been fired for refusing to answer questions about Watergate. These connections showed that the burglary was not a one time job. There was more to it than that.

President Nixon's campaign team tried to cover its tracks by getting rid of key members of the government.

Woodward and Bernstein worked on their own but kept meeting up to compare notes. Bernstein spoke with Hunt in Miami and got information about $89,500 in checks and a $25,500 check deposited into Barker's bank account, Barker being one of the thieves. A Mexican lawyer provided $89,500, while the American businessman Kenneth H. Dahlberg provided $25,000. Dahlberg, Nixon's Midwest campaign director, said the funds were raised for the Nixon campaign. He said he gave the money to Maurice Stans, the CRP Finance Chairman, and Hugh Sloan, the CRP Treasurer.

Woodward pressed for more details, but most of his sources wanted to stay away from the accusations. Even though Stans had not reported the checks to the Federal Elections Division of the General Accounting Office, the GAO, Woodward confirmed that Dahlberg had handed the checks to him. The GAO opened an immediate inquiry as a result. Sloan resigned as CRP treasurer because of the inquiry. The GAO then uncovered a $100,000 slush fund, money set aside for political bribery, run by Gordon Liddy, the CRP's Finance Counsel.

chapters.title: A chain reaction that reshaped American politics
chapters.text:
Bernstein found that many senior White House officials knew about, or were part of, the Watergate scandal. President Nixon denied all the accusations and said no one in his White House knew about the incident. To learn more, Woodward and Bernstein started interviewing members of the CRP. The two journalists found that the FBI had not questioned several important figures who knew that the Democratic Party Headquarters had been bugged.

Woodward made contact with an inside source in the executive branch. He got confirmation from a high ranking government source, known only as Deep Throat, that he had gathered enough material to prove the scandal. Deep Throat and the journalists then agreed that, during their secret meetings, he would not give them new information, but he would confirm or deny anything they brought up. Woodward and Deep Throat first spoke by phone, but as the scandal grew more intense, Deep Throat got nervous and asked for face to face meetings. They set up a signal and met many times to discuss the case.

Deep Throat's role in the Watergate scandal helped clear up a lot of the mystery.

The evidence from Deep Throat led Woodward and Bernstein to Gordon Liddy, who confirmed that Deputy Campaign Director Stuart Magruder, Deputy Director Frederick LaRue, and Campaign Director Clark McGregor were all actively involved in the Watergate scandal.

chapters.title: A newspaper article that shook Nixon’s cabinet
chapters.text:
John Mitchell, the former Attorney General of the United States, was also getting pulled into the controversy. Mitchell had left his role as Nixon's campaign manager, but he remained a key member of the CRP.

The involvement of senior figures deepened the reporters' belief that Watergate was deeply political.

When the reporters spoke with Hugh Sloan, they discovered that five key members of the CRP had access to a large sum that was supposedly used to pay the thieves.

Mitchell, Stans, Magruder, and a White House staff member suspected of being Assistant to the President H. R. Haldeman were among the suspects. Nixon's personal attorney, Herbert Kalmbach, was the final suspect, according to Bernstein. The Washington Post published an article naming these members, which caused a stir. The possibility that President Nixon himself had been involved was starting to take shape.

On September 28, 1972, Bernstein got an anonymous report that a friend of his, Alex Shipley, had been offered a job as a major member of Nixon's CRP. Bernstein called Shipley, and Shipley confirmed that a man named Donald Segretti had reached out, but he had turned the offer down.

Bernstein tried to connect Segretti to Liddy but came up empty. He then contacted Meyers, a Washington Post West Coast reporter, to find Segretti and get information. Meyers located Segretti, but he could not get any useful information out of him. Later, Bernstein learned that Segretti and Kalmbach were also under investigation by the Department of Justice. He also tried, and failed, to link Kalmbach to Segretti.

Through a series of links, Bernstein found that the wiretapping was tied to the election campaign process.

Bernstein learned from another reporter that Kenneth Clawson, the White House Deputy Communications Director, had a letter linked to the Watergate scandal. When Clawson found out that Bernstein knew about the situation and planned to publish it, he got alarmed and promised to deny it if it went public. After gathering enough evidence, the newspaper went public with the FBI's role in the scandal, and with how the people involved worked for Nixon, how they paid for the operations, and so on.

The Washington Post added more detail to the Democratic campaign's allegations. Bernstein was able to connect Segretti to Hunt, and Segretti to Dwight Chapin, Nixon's White House Scheduler and Deputy Assistant. The FBI, through phone records, found that Kalmbach was funding Segretti.

Time magazine's decision to work with the Washington Post reporters and publish a story on the scandal stirred public interest in the whole situation.

Sloan told the reporters that Kalmbach controlled the secret money held by Stans. The publication of the news by top outlets like The Washington Post and Time magazine shook the CRP, and in response, CRP Director of Administration and Personnel Robert Odle issued a three page attack on the Washington Post.

chapters.title: The White House stonewall and the cost of reporting
chapters.text:
Haldeman had created the CRP and acted as a go between for the President and other CRP members tied to the Watergate scandal. Even without hard proof, Haldeman was suspected of playing a role in the secret fund. The President, as expected, denied any involvement, which led Woodward to call Deep Throat again.

Deep Throat had no concrete proof, so he told Woodward he would have to check everything himself. Woodward reached out to Sloan for confirmation. Sloan was not very helpful with tips, but he did put Woodward in touch with an FBI agent who might be able to help. After the agent confirmed that Haldeman was involved, Woodward and Bernstein prepared a piece linking Haldeman to the secret fund. Sloan had been named as a source for their article.

Sloan's lawyer disputed his testimony after the article on Haldeman came out, and Ronald Zeigler, the government's press secretary, condemned the story, making it hard for the two reporters to back up their account.

The Watergate scandal was not enough to swing votes away from Nixon, and he won by a landslide.

Woodward and Bernstein were frustrated. No one seemed ready to talk any more, and their stories barely moved the voters who still supported Nixon for a second term.

Bernstein traveled to Los Angeles to meet with Meyers and Segretti, hoping to learn more. Segretti admitted he worked for Chapin and that Kalmbach paid him. He also said that Haldeman was giving instructions to Chapin.

Bernstein tried to get Segretti to go public and retract his testimony, but he refused. By this point, Nixon had already been re elected President. Bernstein reached out to a third source, who also asked to stay anonymous. Still, there was no solid evidence that could be used to convict anyone. Soon after, the White House barred the Washington Post from attending any social events on its grounds, which only made things worse for the two reporters.

chapters.title: A scandal that changed democracy worldwide
chapters.text:
Alfred Baldwin, the CRP's security guard, was interviewed by the Los Angeles Times, and the interview was tape recorded. On December 19, 1972, Judge Sirica, the Chief Judge of the United States District Court for the District of Columbia, called reporters into the courtroom. He gathered the press to tell them that it was illegal to draw information from a grand jury. Earl Silbert, the lead prosecutor, said that Gordon Liddy was the mastermind behind the operation and had acted on his own, independent of the CRP. Beyond the five thieves, Hunt, and Liddy, Silbert told the court he did not think there was enough evidence to charge anyone else. He believed that adding more defendants would only make the case harder to solve. After the opening statement, Hunt and two other burglars pleaded guilty. Their stories were covered in both the Los Angeles Times and the New York Times. According to the Los Angeles Times, each thief would be paid $1,000 for every month they spent in prison.

At the next court session, Judge Sirica questioned four of the thieves about the money they had been given. They all denied that they had been bribed to plead guilty. One of the thieves, Bernard Barker, said he had no idea how the money had ended up in his account.

Deep Throat was waiting for Woodward when he got back. Deep Throat said he would only confirm facts from then on and would not offer any new information. Woodward then asked about the link between Special Counsel to the President Charles Colson and John Mitchell, the Watergate campaign director. According to Deep Throat, Colson was deeply involved, while Mitchell was less interested.

After long investigations, the Times reported that the Nixon administration had wiretapped official sources to gather information in secret.

Woodward then met with Senator Ervin, the Chairman of the Senate Watergate Committee. Senator Ervin was planning to call everyone to testify, even the President.

chapters.title: Desperate moves that exposed the cover up
chapters.text:
Earl Butz, a United States Secretary, approached Woodward one day and claimed to have served in Army Intelligence. He said he had tracked down a George Washington University student who had once worked for the CRP as a spy. He introduced the source to Woodward as Craig Hillegass, a student who was ready to talk. Hillegass described how the CRP had approached students to spy and report on others, how they were paid in different ways, and that they numbered about 25.

Nixon's attempts to cover his tracks only gave more credibility to Bernstein and Woodward's reporting.

Woodward spoke with other CRP spies after Hillegass's account, and they confirmed the picture. Woodward then spoke with Deep Throat, who said Nixon was prepared to spend $5 million to bring the Washington Post down. Deep Throat also said that Patrick Gray would be named the FBI's new Acting Director. Gray's nomination, Deep Throat believed, would be political, since Gray was aware of the wiretapping of journalists. When pressed, Gray said he had given John Dean, a White House counsel, all the Watergate files he had.

Nixon, sensing things were slipping, held a news conference and announced he would use executive privilege to stop Dean from testifying at the hearings. Later that same day, Gray released a statement saying that Dwight Chapin had called Kalmbach and that Kalmbach had recruited Segretti. This was a major win for the Washington Post, as the public began to trust the paper's reporting.

chapters.title: The only US president ever to resign
chapters.text:
Sam Dash, the Chief Counsel of the Senate Watergate Committee, held a news conference to say he had interviewed James McCord, a former CIA officer, and that McCord would appear in Congressional hearings. Woodward asked Deputy Press Secretary Gerald Warren for a one on one meeting with the President, so he could hear the White House's side directly. As expected, the meeting was denied, and Woodward could not get the White House's account.

In front of the Senate, McCord testified and named Liddy, Mitchell, and Colson. At a news conference, Nixon promised to suspend anyone charged in the Watergate scandal.

Stuart Magruder, the CRP's Deputy Campaign Director, was the next to talk. He accused John Mitchell and John Dean of lying to the Senate about payment schemes, the bugging, and other offenses.

The Washington Post reported that Dean and Mitchell were aware of the whole affair and had helped in the Watergate break in. The paper also reported that Magruder was the source for the information, and that Mitchell and Dean planned to buy the silence of the Watergate defendants.

In a Sunday article, the Post said that Haldeman and John Ehrlichman, a White House counsel and Assistant to the President on Domestic Affairs, were involved in the cover up. The next day, both Haldeman and Ehrlichman resigned. Attorney General Richard Kleindienst joined them, and Dean was fired as well. Richardson Brox was appointed as the new Attorney General, and Nixon spoke to the press about the changes. Evidence found in Ehrlichman's safe showed that 13 government officials and four others had been wiretapped.

By the middle of October 1974, many CRP members had resigned, and Nixon had dismissed the Special Watergate prosecutor. Along with his deputy, the new Attorney General resigned too.

The House Judiciary Committee began the impeachment process against Nixon, who told the State of the Union that he would not resign. In his own mind, he thought he could survive the controversy and stay in office, but the voters who had put him there quickly made clear that he could not. They publicly backed the impeachment effort and showed their anger when the next president, Gerald Ford, pardoned Nixon for his crimes.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
The Watergate scandal was the Nixon administration's illegal attempt to cover up its alleged role in bugging the Democratic National Committee headquarters in Washington. It was an attempt by the administration to listen in on the opposition's election plans by tapping their phones.

The scandal led to Nixon's resignation and sent a strong message to future presidents about how the public views scandal.

The political lesson of Watergate is this. America must never again allow an arrogant, elite guard of political adolescents to bypass the regular party organization and dictate the terms of a national election.

If Woodward and Bernstein had not been brave enough, it is doubtful that Nixon would ever have been charged with a crime. It takes courage to build a reputation as an investigative journalist. Watergate woke the public up to the powers and flaws of presidents, and to why they cannot be trusted without question. It also sent a clear message to future presidents that the people will not sit quietly while a leader ignores the rule of law.

Even if Nixon had not bugged his opponents, he would still have won the election by a wide margin, but he was impatient and surrounded himself with poor advisers.

Watergate shook the United States and much of the rest of the world. The country was already off balance because of Vietnam, economic troubles, political assassinations, and other social and racial crises. The Watergate scandal made a difficult situation worse, yet the United States managed to come out stronger.

Try this

Always stand firm in the truth and avoid being impatient with leadership. Stay away from impatient advisers, because they will lead you off course with clumsy planning.

Read it till the end?
